perturbation.original_text: LHT agrees to comply with all governmental regulations applicable to the Confidential Information, including federal and state securities laws and US Export Control Laws and Regulations.
perturbation.changed_text: LHT agrees to comply with governmental regulations regarding the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the specific mention of 'federal and state securities laws,' the contract could be interpreted as not requiring compliance with insider trading laws, which is a legal contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934, Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: The original text explicitly requires compliance with securities laws, whereas the modified text omits this requirement, potentially creating a loophole for non-compliance with insider trading regulations under Section 10(b) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 and Rule 10b-5.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: This page is better viewed with a JavaScript enabled browser. Document not Found Document not found The document you were looking for does not exist. Click here to close this window, or click here to go back.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly mentions compliance with 'federal and state securities laws,' a crucial aspect of financial regulation. The changed text removes this specific requirement, only stating compliance with 'governmental regulations.' This omission creates a potential loophole, as it could be argued that the contract does not explicitly require adherence to insider trading laws, which are enforced under securities laws. Scraped Law Snippet 2 refers to the 'Securities Exchange Act of 1934,' indicating the importance of securities laws. By removing the explicit mention, the modified contract weakens the obligation to comply with these laws, thus creating a legal contradiction under the Omission Legal Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: Reasonable attorney's fees and costs shall be awarded to HPAC in the event of litigation in which HPAC prevails involving the enforcement or interpretation of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Attorney's fees and costs may be awarded at the discretion of the court.
perturbation.explanation: California law has provisions for awarding attorney's fees to the prevailing party in certain contract disputes. This change introduces ambiguity and removes the guarantee of fee recovery for HPAC, creating a legal contradiction.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1717 provides reciprocal rights to attorney's fees even if a contract only specifies it for one party. By changing the clause to be discretionary, it could be argued that the reciprocal nature of the law is being undermined or obscured, as it removes the guaranteed award of fees to the prevailing party and introduces uncertainty.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1717. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 3. OBLIGATIONS IMPOSED BY LAW [1708 - 1725] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) 1717. (a) In any action on a contract, where the contract specifically provides that attorney’s fees and costs, which are incurred to enforce that contract, shall be awarded either to one of the parties or to the prevailing party, then the party who is determined to be the party prevailing on the contract, whether he or she is the party specified in the contract or not, shall be entitled to reasonable attorney’s fees in addition to other costs. Where a contract provides for attorney’s fees, as set forth above, that provision shall be construed as applying to the entire contract, unless each party was represented by counsel in the negotiation and execution of the contract, and the fact of that representation is specified in the contract. Reasonable attorney’s fees shall be fixed by the court, and shall be an element of the costs of suit. Attorney’s fees provided for by this section shall not be subject to waiver by the parties to any contract which is entered into after the effective date of this section. Any provision in any such contract which provides for a waiver of
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts California Civil Code § 1717. The original text mandated attorney's fees and costs to HPAC if they prevailed. The modified text makes awarding fees discretionary. Snippet 1 explicitly states that 'the party who is determined to be the party prevailing on the contract...shall be entitled to reasonable attorney’s fees'. The original text aligned with this, while the changed text undermines this entitlement by giving the court discretion.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the United States and the State of California, excluding its conflict of laws rules.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the United States, excluding its conflict of laws rules.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the explicit mention of California law could create ambiguity regarding the application of California's specific consumer protection laws that might be relevant to the interpretation or enforcement of the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Consumer Rights Laws (e.g., the Consumer Legal Remedies Act - CLRA)
perturbation.law_explanation: By omitting California law as governing law, the agreement opens itself to potentially circumventing the California Consumer Legal Remedies Act (CLRA), which provides specific protections and remedies to consumers in transactions. While US federal law would still apply, specific CLRA protections could be argued to be inapplicable.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.5. CONSUMERS LEGAL REMEDIES ACT [1750 - 1784] ( Title 1.5 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550. ) CHAPTER 1. General Provisions [1750 - 1756] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550. ) 1750. This title may be cited as the Consumers Legal Remedies Act. (Added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550.)
perturbation.justification: The omission of California law from the governing law clause directly contradicts the intention of the California Consumer Legal Remedies Act (CLRA). The Law Explanation states that by removing California law, the agreement opens itself to potentially circumventing the CLRA. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly identifies the CLRA within California Civil Code (CIV 1750), indicating its established legal standing and applicability within California. This act provides specific protections to consumers, and by omitting California law, these protections are potentially undermined, creating a legal contradiction as defined by 'Omission Legal Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: LHT agrees to prevent the unauthorized disclosure or use of Confidential Information received hereunder using the same degree of care employed to protect its own confidential information, but in no event less than a reasonable standard of care.
perturbation.changed_text: LHT agrees to prevent the unauthorized disclosure or use of Confidential Information received hereunder using a reasonable standard of care.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'the same degree of care employed to protect its own confidential information' lowers the standard of care required. If LHT normally uses a very high degree of care, then omitting this is a contradiction, as it implicitly weakens protections and could make it easier to violate California's trade secret laws if the information qualifies as a trade secret.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. Removing the clause that LHT should use the same care as it uses for its own confidential information lowers the bar and could be seen as insufficient effort to protect trade secrets, potentially contradicting CUTSA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modification weakens the standard of care required to protect confidential information. The original text mandated using 'the same degree of care employed to protect its own confidential information, but in no event less than a reasonable standard of care.' The changed text only requires 'a reasonable standard of care,' potentially allowing for a lower level of protection than what LHT normally uses for its own confidential information. The Law Explanation states CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets, and the lowered standard could be viewed as insufficient effort, contradicting CUTSA. Scraped Law Snippet 1 cites the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) in Civil Code section 3426, indicating the importance of trade secret protection in California. The omission directly impacts the level of effort to protect trade secrets.
